summary: Gruber responds to Joshua Topolsky's harsh dismissal of the iPad Pro as a laptop replacement, agreeing with most of Topolsky's specific criticisms while arguing he's fundamentally wrong in his framing. Gruber acknowledges that for people like himself and Topolsky, a MacBook is clearly the better work device. However, he argues that the iPad Pro with Smart Keyboard genuinely is the right work device for millions of other people. He contends that judging the iPad by laptop standards is as unfair as judging the Mac by iPad standards. He revisits his 2010 argument that the iPad's core value proposition is doing less in order to do what it does better and more simply.
key_points: Topolsky's specific criticisms of the iPad Pro are largely accurate, but his conclusion that it can't replace a laptop is framed incorrectly as universal rather than personal
The iPad Pro is not the right device for power users like Gruber and Topolsky, but it genuinely serves millions of other people as their primary work device
Criticizing the iPad for not being a laptop is structurally identical to criticizing a Mac for not being as simple as an iPad — both miss the point
The iPad's fundamental design philosophy is to do less so that what it does, it does better and more elegantly
Apple continues making both MacOS and MacBook hardware precisely because different users have legitimately different needs
money_quotes: I agree with almost every single word in Topolsky's thread — but I also think he's completely wrong.

I can say without hesitation that the iPad Pro is not the work device for me. I can also say without hesitation that the iPad Pro with a Smart Keyboard is the work device for millions of other people.
A MacBook is better in some ways; an iPad is better in others.

"Imagine a computer, but everything works worse than you expect" is no more fair as criticism of the iPad than a statement like "Imagine an iPad but everything is more complicated and there's always a jumble of dozens of overlapping windows cluttering the screen" would be as criticism of the Mac.
The central conceit of the iPad is that it's a portable computer that does less — and because it does less, what it does do, it does better, more simply, and more elegantly.
It's the heaviness of the Mac that allows iOS to remain light.
key_insight: Agreeing with every specific criticism of the iPad Pro while rejecting the overall conclusion illustrates that the real error is treating personal workflow preferences as universal truths about a device's value.
